question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a hex block number with leading zeros (e.g., '0x0000000000000001') to extractNumericBlock(), causing it to parse correctly but potentially bypass any length-based validation in upstream middleware, and then use this to request block 1 (genesis+1) which may have a very large consensusEnd - consensusStart gap representing a 500%+ block time delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a timestamp equal to the consensusStart of a block (rather than consensusEnd) to findByTimestamp(), and since the query uses 'consensusEnd >= timestamp', receive the correct block, but if the block's consensusEnd - consensusStart is very large (indicating a slow block), the service returns it without alerting callers to the delay, hiding a 500%+ block time violation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on findByBlockType() calls to send a high volume of requests for the LATEST block, causing the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_LATEST cache to be under heavy read pressure, and if the cache implementation has a thundering herd problem on expiry, cause a burst of simultaneous database queries that delay block resolution beyond 500% of the average block time?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a timestamp that is exactly at the boundary between two consecutive record files (consensusEnd of block N equals consensusStart of block N+1), causing findByTimestamp() to return block N+1 instead of block N, making the computed block time for block N appear to be zero and for block N+1 appear to span two blocks, falsely indicating a 500%+ delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a block number that is exactly the current chain tip index to findByBlockType(), receive the latest block, and then immediately send the same request again after the cache TTL expires during a real network slowdown, causing the cache to serve a block whose consensusEnd is more than 500% of the average block time behind the actual chain tip?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findByTimestamp() returns the block with the smallest consensusEnd that is >= the given timestamp, meaning if there is a large gap between two blocks (e.g., 500%+ of average block time), a timestamp in the middle of the gap will return the later block, making the gap invisible to callers who only see the returned block's timestamps?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a block tag of 'LATEST' (uppercase) to BlockType.of(), which converts to lowercase before matching, correctly resolving to BlockType.LATEST, but if the caller expects case-sensitive matching and the cache key is case-sensitive, cause a cache miss and a fresh database query that reveals a stale latest block with a 500%+ delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a timestamp of 1 (the smallest positive nanosecond timestamp) to findByTimestamp(), causing the query to return the genesis block (the first block with consensusEnd >= 1), and if the genesis block has a very large consensusEnd value (e.g., representing a real timestamp in nanoseconds since epoch), make block-time calculations appear to show a massive delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Caching annotation on findByTimestamp() which performs both @Cacheable and @CachePut operations, by sending a timestamp query that populates the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX cache with a block index mapping, and then use that cached index in a subsequent findByBlockType() call to retrieve a block that is significantly older than the current chain tip, simulating a 500%+ block time delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a block number of 0 as a decimal string to BlockType.of(), which parses it as a numeric block (not EARLIEST singleton), causing findByIndex(0) to be called instead of findEarliest(), and if the index-based query returns a different block than the earliest-ordered query (e.g., due to index reassignment via updateIndex()), return a wrong block whose timestamps indicate a 500%+ delay?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of upper-bound validation on the timestamp parameter in findByTimestamp() to pass a timestamp far in the future (e.g., year 2100 in nanoseconds), causing the query to return empty Optional for every request, making all transaction lookups fail and simulating a complete network freeze where no block can be found?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/RecordFileService.java -> Scope: High: Temporary freezing of network transactions by delaying one block by 500% or more of the average block time of the preceding 24 hours beyond standard difficulty adjustments'] [Function: findByBlockType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a hex block number '0x7fffffffffffffff' (Long.MAX_VALUE) to extractNumericBlock(), which parses successfully without overflow, causing findByIndex(Long.MAX_VALUE) to return empty Optional, and if the caller interprets empty as 'block not yet produced', create a false impression that the network has not produced any block at that height, simulating a freeze?
